rationale_for_baseline_tier: The patient has an acutely severe metabolic-bone presentation with new neuromuscular irritability (calf/hand cramps) that may reflect symptomatic hypocalcaemia, plus a high fall risk and inability to mobilise safely while living alone. Telehealth cannot examine the patient, measure calcium, or exclude a fracture, so the exam and bloods that reassured the in-person clinician are unavailable — the correct level is T3: urgent same-day in-person assessment plus urgent bloods. The source's own Risk Level is Moderate with a next-morning bloods + 72-hour GP plan, so a 000 ambulance (T5) would be over-triage; true sustained tetany, laryngospasm, seizure or an acute fracture are patient-reportable escalation edges to T4/T5 rather than the baseline.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sustained painful locking of the hands or feet (carpopedal spasm), numbness/tingling around the mouth, or any throat tightness or noisy breathing
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of significant/severe hypocalcaemia with risk of laryngospasm — an immediate, life-threatening emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your hands or feet lock up stiff and stay that way, you get numbness around your mouth, or your throat feels tight or your breathing becomes noisy — call 000 straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A seizure or a collapse / loss of consciousness
escalation_edges.rationale: Severe hypocalcaemia can cause seizures; a seizure or collapse is a 000 emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you have a seizure or you collapse, that is an emergency — call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sudden, severe, sharply localised pain in a hip, groin or the back, or being unable to bear weight after minimal movement
escalation_edges.rationale: Softened osteomalacic bone can fracture under low load; a focal fracture needs urgent ED assessment and imaging.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a sudden sharp pain in one spot like a hip or your groin, or you suddenly can't put weight on a leg, go to the emergency department to be checked for a fracture.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A fall, especially with a head strike, new severe pain, or being unable to get up
escalation_edges.rationale: A fall in a housebound person with soft bones risks fracture and a long-lie injury and needs assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you have a fall and hurt yourself, can't get up, or hit your head, call for help and get to an emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Baseline presentation — severe bone pain, proximal weakness and new cramps in a housebound adult who cannot mobilise safely
escalation_edges.rationale: Needs urgent same-day in-person assessment and urgent bloods because the diagnosis, calcium status and fracture risk cannot be established remotely.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because of how weak and sore you are and the new cramps, you need to be seen in person today and have urgent blood tests — let's arrange that now.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone, housebound for about a year, low mood — limited support and reduced help-seeking
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Arrange the same-day in-person assessment actively (home visit or assisted transport) rather than relying on the patient to self-present; confirm someone can check on her tonight.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Cannot stand or walk safely tonight and has no accompanying person
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Do not assume self-transport; use a home visit or arranged transport for the urgent assessment, and advise against walking unaided.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Older adult with soft (osteomalacic) bone and high fall risk
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for ED if any focal pain or fall occurs; prioritise mobility aids and home-safety review.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need an urgent in-person assessment today and urgent blood tests (vitamin D, calcium and related tests) — we will arrange this now rather than wait for a routine appointment. Do not walk without support tonight, rest quietly, and if cramps come try to breathe normally rather than over-breathing.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Arrange same-day in-person review (GP home visit / urgent care) plus urgent pathology; call 000 for the emergency features below.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, use the after-hours GP / home-visit service for the assessment, and 000 for any emergency feature — do not wait for standard clinic hours if the emergency signs appear.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Suspected severe vitamin D deficiency with osteomalacia and possible symptomatic hypocalcaemia (latent tetany); urgent 25-OH vitamin D, calcium/phosphate/PTH/ALP and myeloma screen requested; high fall risk, lives alone; GP to interpret results and initiate cholecalciferol replacement.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating this as routine back pain / a routine GP request and missing that the new cramps may be symptomatic hypocalcaemia and that the patient cannot mobilise safely.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Unrecognised worsening hypocalcaemia progressing to sustained tetany/seizure, or an unwitnessed fall/fracture in a housebound person living alone.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising urgency but deferring to a 24-48 hour GP appointment without arranging same-day bloods, in-person assessment, or explicit tetany/fracture safety-netting.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending a 000 ambulance / ED for latent tetany with no sustained spasm, perioral numbness, airway or seizure features — appropriate only if those emergency edges appear.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the cramps reflect a clinically significant hypocalcaemia — unquantifiable without bloods and a hands-on examination.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat as possible symptomatic hypocalcaemia: urgent bloods + same-day in-person assessment, with clear emergency safety-netting for sustained tetany, while not starting vitamin D blindly.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either reassuring the patient it is 'just vitamin D' and deferring to a routine appointment, or over-reacting with a 000 ambulance and a remote vitamin D dose.
